title: Sealift: Tomorrow’s Fight Must Be Uncrewed-But Not Unattended
description: General Prize Essay Contest—First Prize. Autonomous cargo vessels are the sealift expansion the United States needs now.
text:
A major conflict looms on the horizon, and U.S. sealift is in a dismal state. In conflict, U.S. Transportation Command expects to move 90 percent of the material needed to project force by sea, but U.S. wartime cargo capacity (encompassing vessels of Military Sealift Command [MSC], the Maritime Administration’s [MarAd’s] National Defense Reserve Fleet, and enrolled Commercial Sealift Support Program vessels) is insufficient to the projected tonnage and duration requirements, even before expected combat losses.1 Worse, it is unlikely that even the most aggressive reinvigoration options for U.S. shipbuilding could build the needed additional capacity—barrels, deadweight tonnage, car equivalent units (CEUs), and 20-foot equivalent units (TEUs)—in time.2
Further limiting sealift capacity is the shortage of physically able, willing U.S. mariners who can be trained to operate and repair the current fleet, let alone an expanded one.3 Much like the difficulty with building more ships, recruiting enough mariners and training them in the skills required by today’s automated vessels would take more years than the decade has left.
How, then, does the United States “project and sustain combat credible forces at scale and velocity” anywhere about the globe before the end of the decade?4 One possible answer is to quickly build an emergency cargo fleet similar to the thousands of Liberty and Victory ships produced in the 1940s, but this time as lightly attended, unmanned cargo vessels that deploy in convoys formed about conventional ships.
Ongoing USV Projects
Both public and private enterprises have driven research to remove mariners from vessels, to both reduce operating costs and shift limited personnel away from the “dirty, dangerous, and dull” operations.5 The field is evolving rapidly, but even impressive current projects lack sufficient cargo capacity to enable sealift in oceans experiencing unrestricted warfare. Ghost Fleet Overlord, the No Manning Required Ship (NOMARS), the Marine Corps’ Long Range Unmanned Surface Vessel, and recently released indications of the Future USV program focus heavily on providing relatively lightweight sensor or weapon platforms over cargo capacity.6 While a few projects, such as Leidos’s Sea Archer, are beginning to tackle contested last-mile logistics, meaningful autonomous transoceanic capacity remains a distant prospect absent from realistic discussions.7 The largest publicly disclosed cargo carrier to date, Austal’s autonomous-enabled expeditionary fast transport vessel (EPF), carries roughly 600 tons—around the equivalent of 40 loaded containers—when the transoceanic need will be measured in tens of thousands of barrels, CEUs, and TEUs per month.8
Upscaling to meet this need is a major technological challenge somewhat separate from existing combatant-focused projects. U.S. government agencies—particularly MarAd and MSC—should pursue their own lightly attended unmanned cargo vessel designs that prioritize displacement and speed over intervention-free duration. This is the most likely path to ensuring enough ammunition, food, spare parts, and fuel in theater.
Autonomous-Augmented Sealift: A Vision
A lightly attended uncrewed cargo vessel would expand the available tonnage without requiring a proportional expansion of trained mariners. In this vision, a slight variance exists between transoceanic employment (moving goods from the U.S. mainland to a theater) and intratheater distribution, but possibilities abound.
To move equipment between highly developed, automated port facilities in or near theater, conventional, manned vessels would form the core of convoys while shepherding a flock of 600- to 800-foot uncrewed cargo vessels. These would likely include existing MSC hulls with embarked helicopters for rapid personnel transfers. Combatants that escort the convoys may also comprise other MarAd and Cable Ship Security Program vessels for maximum throughput against unrestricted warfare. The manned vessels would carry additional maintainers devoted to making rounds of each of the autonomous vessels every few days and addressing issues the automation identified but could not self-correct. Maintenance currently regarded as intermediate- to depot-level could be conducted either underway in shifts or deferred to a port, with a minimum expectation of 60 days underway between pierside work periods.
For moving equipment intratheater to smaller, less-developed ports, handy-sized autonomous cargo ships should also be paired with a conventionally crewed mothership or tender. If the distances are short enough, these vessels could transit between feeder ports autonomously, though this would increase the risk of hijacking or enemy capture. Intratheater ships could be as small as EPFs or as long as 500 feet and equipped with their own cranes, pumping equipment, or ramps as appropriate.
For a large-volume approach to unimproved islands, autonomous vessels (roughly resembling existing Navy landing craft, utility—LCUs) would unload onto the beach using a ramp that could be designed to float off a purpose-built crewed vessel. The crewed mothership would be similar to the Seahorse vessels—heavy lift shell ships that would float off smaller, self-propelled vessels. On reaching a node serving several small ports, the mothership would recover the smaller vessels back to the well deck for the longer transit.9 These 100- to 200-foot shuttle vessels would be entirely uncrewed, with the mothership crew performing maintenance during transits.
In these employment scenarios, the autonomous cargo vessels would act as the surface version of “loyal wingmen.” The unmanned cargo ships would need to meet a greater reliability standard than currently exists for large vessels, but not the ambitious months-to-years goal for most current USV projects. This critical adjustment would enable upscaling USVs to meaningful cargo tonnage to be ready for the near-term fight.
The Imperative
The existing mariner shortage demands creative solutions. The maritime industry is working toward at least a limited application of autonomous cargo vessels, incentivized in part by an anticipated reduction in operating costs. Left to its own devices, the free market is likely to eventually yield that capability. However, commercial-specific hurdles, such as national and international regulatory frameworks and liability concerns, combined with technological challenges, mean that profitable large-scale crewless cargo carriage is decades away. Governments need to develop more immediate solutions for noncommercial applications.
Lightly attended autonomous vessels would spread the expertise of the relatively small number of U.S. mariners (estimated around 12,000 in 2017, compared with the pre–World War II number of 55,000) across more hulls, while putting fewer mariners at risk per hull.10 Their adoption also would address the reality that training a four- to fivefold increase in the number of oceangoing mariners would be even more difficult today than it was in the early 1940s, when mariners were being killed at several times the rate of their armed forces counterparts.11 Even sticking to a design whose equipment and electronic controls are simple by today’s standards, the time to train a new marine engineer to a proficiency that will not exacerbate mechanical downtime is measured in years: one and a half during World War II but at least three at present—and only after stripping out classes included purely for college accreditation. Worse, the three-year estimate assumes sufficient recruitment of civilian mariners willing and physically able to sail into theater in the first place.
And strategists must assume a near-peer enemy will pursue some degree of unrestricted warfare on civilian shipping, as demonstrated by both history and current events. For several weeks in the spring of 1942, U-boats sank nearly one tanker per day in the Atlantic and Caribbean.12 An average of more than 230 U.S. and supporting foreign-flagged merchant ships were sunk per year from 1941 to 1945.13 Such attrition would be untenable today. Though they carry far more cargo than their 20th-century predecessors, only 170 U.S. merchant ships have been deemed “militarily useful” by the Department of Transportation.14 Because of their larger size, each loss would proportionally be far more costly than a merchant loss in the 1940s. Terrorist attacks and major maritime mishaps in recent years demonstrate the inherent vulnerability of cargo vessels—better to have some that can be lost without commensurate loss of life and expertise.
The Power Problem with Upscaling
With the current autonomous surface projects unlikely to aid sealift soon, an interim technological design is needed for tomorrow’s fight. Keen observers of USV research note the boundaries on current viable prototypes: an upward limit on vessel size and displacement, determined by a combination of the amount and duration of propulsion power required in an unattended state. The stipulated objectives drive a requirement to generate power on board.
For example, a small vessel using wind for propulsion and solar for minor sail adjustment could operate unattended almost indefinitely. For a 100-foot vessel carrying some sensors and a single weapon system, a month or slightly longer has been achieved. But the horsepower curve is increasingly demanding, and upscaling to sealift-level displacement moving at more than 20 knots requires tremendous power that cannot currently be sustained by the relatively simple and reliable propulsion of most USVs: electric motors powered by batteries recharged on shore. Generating power on board without frequent human intervention is orders of magnitude more difficult, which is why the largest successful autonomous diesel vessel trials to date have been the EPF and midsize offshore support vessels capable of carrying only a handful of containers. For scale, the Navy’s prototype Nomad had four 1,800-hp engines, while an 850-foot, 5,000-TEU container ship needs more than 30,000 hp to make 24 knots.15 The slow-speed diesel engines commonly used for this purpose are five stories tall.
Until battery power density improves tremendously, transoceanic cargo vessels and their handy-sized
interisland counterparts are most likely to be diesel-powered. Alternative gaseous fuels, such as liquefied natural gas and ammonia, are temperamental in combustion and require cryogenic storage with all its associated equipment. Nuclear propulsion, likewise, is far too fraught with environmental concerns for unmanned operation near U.S. or friendly shores, let alone undefended in theater. While these factors may change, or an entirely new fuel option may become viable in future research, the least-intensive option currently available for generating power on board is petroleum distillates.
Even this option is not without its challenges. While several diesel-engine designs have achieved a 30-day unattended operation certification in recent years, their required support-system components lag far behind. As one USV developer quipped, “No one’s ever met a purifier that didn’t need a lot of love.” Maintaining valves is a major challenge with any liquid or gaseous fuel system. Prone to failure in hot, vibrating environments, a valve on an unmanned vessel that failed open would allow fuel to keep flowing to a leak or rupture. Conversely, if a valve failed closed, the USV’s engines could die.
Redundancy is the simplest counter to component failure when there are no humans on board to make repairs, but this cannot be applied to valves without adding tremendous weight in redundant piping. Moreover, redundant components are additional points of potential failure.
For now, the best option for autonomous cargo vessels is an uncrewed but periodically attended propulsion plant. Until subsystem components become sufficiently reliable, USV designers would not have to devise engineering solutions for every potential failure.
Design Tenets for an Unmanned Cargo Fleet
For autonomous-augmented sealift to be successful, the shipbuilding administration must emulate the Liberty and Victory ships. They were simple, even for the late 1930s, using a single piston-type steam engine and generating direct-current electricity. But each hull could be built in a matter of days during peak production, and mariners could quickly learn to maintain and repair them. By 1944, production lines had switched to steam turbine Victory-class ships to remedy some of the Liberty class’s vulnerabilities (and increasing speed by one third) while keeping the ships simple to build and quick to learn.16 With that build-improve-build model in mind, below are key attributes to enable rapid mass production, realize the greatest benefit per hull, and achieve maximum reliability with today’s technology in the first generation of autonomous cargo vessels:
Simplicity: Autonomous cargo vessels of all sizes should be built rapidly, be nominally expendable (at least more so than their crewed counterparts), and be easy to build and maintain. Avoid exquisite solutions wherever possible, provided the vessel can operate autonomously without intervention for at least five days.
Hull form: Maximize cargo capacity while meeting an 18–22 knot cruising speed (tankers possibly excepted). Design for rapid construction in multiple yards, even if it requires shifting away from military standards and toward less-stringent commercial rules. Survivability is not as important when life is not at stake. Remove all superstructure except as required for navigation sensors and personnel boarding.
Auxiliary equipment: Avoid any equipment not strictly necessary for power and propulsion. Assume that personnel will not be on board for more than a 12-hour shift and will be rotated to and from a crewed vessel if underway repairs require greater duration. Even toilet facilities should be spare, direct-overboard discharge, as most operations will be farther than three nautical miles from shore. Avoid most if not all environmental equipment, such as exhaust scrubbers and ballast-water treatment. Even long-standard oily water separators are not needed for unattended reliability. Pump slops ashore or to a crewed tender for processing.
Propulsion: Avoid power-dense engines that tend to require finely tuned digital-control systems. Less-complicated, mostly mechanically controlled engines tolerate more fuel impurities and have fewer control failures.
Navigation: Design for primarily autonomous movement with occasional destination updates, movement constantly operated remotely from the lead vessel, or a combination of the two. Enable last-command autonomous execution in the event the lead crewed vessel is lost or rendered immobile, to maximize odds of cargo delivery. Entering and exiting port may require dedicated mariner remote control and extra tugs, but physical onboard controls should be limited to disincentivize piracy and capture.
Quantity: Build more hulls than needed to account both for losses and the inevitable pierside maintenance intervals. Downtime ratios will depend on installed equipment and the proportion of underway maintainers to hulls.
Support: Establish shoreside facilities in theater to conduct pierside maintenance to minimize transit time for vessels that do not frequently visit major U.S. ports.
Just as Victory ships built on Liberty ships’ lessons, a first attempt at lightly attended autonomous cargo ships would undoubtedly provide revelations for a follow-on class requiring even less human intervention and may even hasten the development of requisite subcomponent technology.
The Best Path for a Nation Short on Time
Many ideas have been proposed to address the shortage of merchant hulls and mariners to crew them. Buying ships has an appeal, though some have noted such a course would outpace the development of the mariners to sail them.17 Bolstering sealift capacity with prize ships is another proposition, but these vessels would be difficult to learn, hard to troubleshoot and supply with spare parts, may contain booby traps, and are unlikely to be seized in the required numbers and types.18 If major conflict is indeed on the horizon, there is insufficient time to build and crew a conventional merchant fleet.
The Navy, U.S. Transportation Command, MarAd, and MSC must not wait for the current, combatant-focused USV programs to upscale to sealift cargo capacity. A separate building program is needed that relaxes the requirements for intervention-free duration in favor of maximizing deadweight tonnage and speed of production. Periodically attended autonomous cargo vessels are the sealift expansion the United States needs now to have sufficient power projection capability for the near-term fight.
